1: Lekhu ve-nashuva el-YHWH -- 'Come, let us return to the LORD.' Ch 6 opens with Israel's response to Hosea 5:15 -- the seeking that YHWH awaited. Whether this is genuine repentance or shallow liturgy is the great question the chapter raises (Hosea 6:4 casts doubt on its depth). Nashuva (H7725, let us return) -- the first person plural cohortative of shub, the key repentance word. Ki hu taraf ve-yirpenu -- 'He has torn us, but he will heal us.' The acknowledgment that YHWH is the source of both the wound and the healing. Yakh ve-yachavsenu -- 'he has wounded us, but he will bind up our wounds.' The verb chavash (H2280, to bind up/bandage) is used of medical care; YHWH as the physician who diagnoses, wounds, and heals.
2: Yechayenu mi-yomayim ba-yom ha-shelishi yeqimenu -- 'After two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up.' The temporal formula -- two days, then the third day -- has generated enormous NT interest because of its resonance with the resurrection. Yechayenu (H2421, he will revive us/give us life) and yeqimenu (H6965, raise us up) -- both verbs are used in the NT for resurrection. Whatever the original referent (the short period of national judgment before covenant restoration), the language anticipates the resurrection idiom. Paul's citation in 1 Corinthians 15:4 ('he was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures') draws on this broader OT pattern of third-day divine intervention and vindication (cf. Genesis 22:4; Exodus 19:11; Joshua 1:11; 2 Kings 20:5). Ve-nichyeh le-fanav -- 'and we will live before him.' Covenant life lived in YHWH's presence: the goal of the return.
3: Ve-neda nirdefa ladat et-YHWH -- 'Let us press on to know the LORD.' Radaf (H7291, to pursue/press on after) -- the verb of pursuit used elsewhere for chasing enemies is here applied to the knowledge of God. Ke-shachar nakhon motzao -- 'his appearing is as certain as the dawn.' The dawn (shachar, H7837) is the reliable daily renewal -- YHWH's self-revelation is as dependable as the sunrise. Ve-yavo chameishem la-matar -- 'He will come to us like the rain.' The rain (matar) and the latter rain (maqosh) are the covenant blessings of agricultural provision (Deuteronomy 11:14; Joel 2:23). YHWH's coming is like the seasonal rains that make the land fruitful -- the comparison grounds divine revelation in the covenant agricultural promise.
4: Mah easeh lekha Efrayim mah easeh lekha Yehudah -- 'What shall I do with you, Ephraim? What shall I do with you, Judah?' The divine perplexity -- not ignorance but exasperation. The rhetorical question expresses the pathos of YHWH's love frustrated by repeated covenant failures. Ve-chasedkhem ke-anan-boqer -- 'your love is like a morning cloud.' Chesed is here Israel's reciprocal covenant love (not YHWH's) -- and it evaporates like the morning cloud (anan-boqer). Ve-ka-tal mashkim holekh -- 'like the morning dew that disappears early.' The dew is beautiful, refreshing, and transient. Israel's repentance (Hosea 6:1-3) is precisely this: lovely in expression, gone before noon. This verse casts retrospective doubt on the sincerity of Hosea 6:1-3 and confirms that the issue is not a lack of religious language but a lack of covenant depth.
5: Al-ken chatzavti ba-neviim haragtim be-imrei fi -- 'Therefore I have hewn them down by the prophets; I have slain them by the words of my mouth.' The prophetic word as the instrument of divine judgment: YHWH's words spoken through the prophets are the sword that slays and the chisel that hews. Ve-mishpatekha or yetze -- 'and my judgments shine forth like the light.' Mishpat (justice/judgment) made visible and clear through the prophetic proclamation. The prophets' devastating words are the light that exposes what the morning clouds of ephemeral repentance conceal.
6: Ki chesed chafatzti ve-lo zavach -- 'For I desire covenant loyalty and not sacrifice.' The most programmatic verse in Hosea -- quoted twice by Jesus (Matthew 9:13; Hosea 12:7). Chafatz (H2654, to delight in/desire) -- YHWH's desire is not for ritual performance but for covenant relationship expressed in loyalty. Chesed (H2617, covenant love/loyalty/steadfast love) -- the term that Hosea has used throughout (Hosea 2:19; Hosea 4:1) as the missing virtue. Ve-lo zavach (not sacrifice) -- this is not an absolute rejection of the sacrificial system but a statement of priority: when chesed is absent, sacrifice is unacceptable. Ve-daath Elohim mi-olot -- 'the knowledge of God rather than burnt offerings.' Daath Elohim is the relational knowledge of the covenant that Hosea has established as the central virtue (Hosea 4:1, 6). Jesus applies this verse twice: to defend his association with sinners (Matthew 9:13 -- mercy is the priority) and to defend his disciples' grain-plucking on the Sabbath (Matthew 12:7 -- covenant love ranks above ritual regulation). The verse becomes a hermeneutical key for how the new covenant reads the old.
7: Ve-hemah ke-Adam avru verit -- 'But like Adam they have transgressed the covenant.' Adam can be read as 'humanity' in general or as a specific place name; the MKT reads it as 'Adam' (the first human) and thus as a typological comparison: Israel has repeated Adam's covenant transgression. The covenant at Sinai mirrors the covenant at Eden: both involved prohibition, both were violated by human choice, both resulted in expulsion from the divine presence. Sham bagdu vi -- 'there they have dealt faithlessly with me.' The faithlessness is geographically specific -- at the covenant sites (Adam, Gilead) where covenant was made and violated.
8: Gilad qiryat poalei aven -- 'Gilead is a city of evildoers.' Gilead was a Transjordanian region associated with violence and political instability. Aquvah mi-dam -- 'stained with blood.' The city stained with blood (perhaps the murders of 2 Kings 15:25-30 in the territory) is the concrete instantiation of the social breakdown described in Hosea 4:2. The idolatry of the cult (Hosea 4-5) and the violence of the social order are twin symptoms of the same covenant failure.
9: U-khe-chavei ish gedudim chakeh kohen -- 'As raiders lie in wait for someone, so do bands of priests.' The priests have become organized criminals -- not merely corrupt but actively predatory. Derekh yirtzachu shekhemah -- 'they murder on the road to Shechem.' Shechem was a cultic city and the site of covenant assemblies (Josh 24). The road to the covenant assembly site has become the road of priestly ambush. Ki zimah asu -- 'they commit outrages.' Zimah (H2154, outrage/crime/lewdness) -- the strongest vocabulary for moral atrocity. The priestly institution has become a criminal enterprise.
10: Be-veit Yisrael raiti shaeruriyah -- 'I have seen a horrible thing in the house of Israel.' Shaeruriyah (H8186, horrible thing/atrocity) -- a word reserved for the most extreme covenant violations (cf. Jeremiah 5:30; Hosea 23:14). Sham zenut le-Efrayim -- 'Ephraim's prostitution is there.' The spiritual fornication has become so normalized that it is simply 'there' -- part of the landscape of the northern kingdom's religious life. Nitma Yisrael -- 'Israel is defiled.' The defilement (tame) is complete: the covenant people has become ritually impure from within through its own idolatry.
11: Gam-Yehudah shat qatzir lakh -- 'Also, O Judah, a harvest is appointed for you.' The harvest (qatzir, H7105) can be positive (restoration, as in Hosea 2:22-23) or negative (judgment, as in Joel 3:13; Revelation 14:15). Here the ambiguity is intentional: Judah's harvest-appointment comes when YHWH restores (be-shuvi shevut ammi) the fortunes of his people. The verse bridges the judgment of Hosea 5-6 and the restoration that the book promises: Judah's 'harvest' is tied to the broader divine restoration of the covenant people. The chapter ends on the threshold between judgment and hope -- the characteristic Hosea position.
